title: Introduction and Research Background
text: This review synthesizes evidence from 15 studies examining pedestrian safety risk assessment, accident analysis, safety management evaluation, crossing behavior observation, and perception and preference surveys. Findings are organized around 69 discrete claims derived from a structured comparison matrix. The reviewed evidence employs empirical/statistical analysis, mixed-methods observational field studies, and microscopic agent-based simulation as primary analytical frameworks. A persistent challenge in this literature is the heterogeneity in how studies operationalize crossing behavior constructs, report metrics, and handle context specificity—factors that collectively limit direct cross-study comparison. This review structures its analysis around methodological families, task-level performance patterns, metric reporting practices, and verified research gaps, moving beyond siloed paper summaries toward structured evidence synthesis. The review is organized as follows: Section 2 examines methodological approaches and their representational trade-offs; Section 3 addresses task-level findings and performance patterns; Section 4 evaluates metric reporting and comparability; Section 5 identifies verified research gaps and implications; and Section 6 provides a field-level synthesis and research priorities.

title: Methodological Families in Pedestrian Crossing Research
text: The empirical/statistical analysis family constitutes a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. This approach offers distinct representational advantages and is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. However, evidence suggests reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The mixed-methods observational field study and survey research family similarly represents a primary strand, combining behavioral observation with self-reported data to capture both objective crossing patterns and subjective decision-making factors. The microscopic simulation agent-based family represents a secondary methodological strand, enabling controlled experimentation across hypothetical scenarios. The microscopic simulation social force model family captures emergent group behaviors, including collective waiting dynamics, though it requires careful parameter calibration and demonstrates limited generalizability to contexts with substantially different group compositions. The microscopic traffic simulation behavioral modeling family provides parameters directly interpretable for traffic engineering and policy purposes. Across these methodological families, a clear tension emerges between interpretive richness and predictive performance. Structural models—including discrete choice and social force approaches—yield parameters directly applicable to traffic engineering and policy, whereas data-driven approaches achieve higher predictive accuracy on specific benchmark datasets but lack comparable policy utility. No single paradigm has been validated across the full range of pedestrian populations, traffic environments, and signal configurations represented in this corpus.

title: Pedestrian Red Light Running and Violation Modeling
text: This section synthesizes findings from 15 papers examining pedestrian red light running behavior, including safety risk assessment, accident analysis, and crossing behavior observation. The comparative evidence matrix structures claims, metrics, and limitations to support structured analysis of violation occurrence patterns and contributing factors. A key observation is that empirical/statistical analysis and mixed-methods observational field study approaches dominate this corpus, though significant heterogeneity in reporting practices limits direct cross-study comparison. The reviewed literature identifies vehicle arrival timing, signal phase duration, peer behavior, and perceived urgency as primary determinants of red light running behavior, with downstream implications for pedestrian safety outcomes.

title: Microscopic Simulation Models for Pedestrian Behavior
text: This section synthesizes findings from 15 papers employing microscopic simulation approaches to model pedestrian crossing behavior, including agent-based and social force models. The comparative evidence matrix structures claims, metrics, and limitations to support structured analysis of simulation-based predictions. A key observation is that simulation approaches increasingly supplement empirical methods in this corpus, though validation against observed field data remains inconsistent across studies. The literature demonstrates the utility of microscopic simulation for exploring hypothetical scenarios and parameter sensitivity, while noting challenges in calibrating model parameters to reflect real-world behavioral heterogeneity.

title: Autonomous Vehicle-Pedestrian Interaction and Communication
text: This section synthesizes findings from 15 papers examining pedestrian behavior in interaction with autonomous vehicles, including crossing decision-making, trust formation, and communication mechanism effectiveness. The comparative evidence matrix structures claims, metrics, and limitations to support structured analysis of human-machine interaction dynamics. A key observation is that the emerging literature on autonomous vehicle-pedestrian interaction reveals significant gaps in understanding how explicit and implicit communication signals influence crossing decisions. The reviewed evidence highlights the need for context-sensitive models that account for varying levels of vehicle automation and communication modality.

title: Behavioral Factors and Psychological Determinants
text: This section synthesizes findings from 15 papers examining psychological and behavioral determinants of pedestrian crossing decisions, including risk perception, social influence, and habit formation. The comparative evidence matrix structures claims, metrics, and limitations to support structured analysis of individual-level decision factors. A key observation is that psychological determinants—such as time pressure, risk tolerance, and perceived safety—significantly moderate crossing behavior but are inconsistently measured across the reviewed literature. The evidence suggests that integrating behavioral theories more systematically would enhance predictive model validity and intervention design.

title: Mobile Phone Distraction and Visual Attention
text: This section synthesizes findings from 15 papers examining the effects of mobile phone use on pedestrian visual attention and crossing behavior, including distraction duration, gaze patterns, and safety outcomes. The comparative evidence matrix structures claims, metrics, and limitations to support structured analysis of distraction-related risks. A key observation is that mobile phone distraction significantly increases crossing latency and error rates, with text-based communication presenting greater risks than voice-based interaction. The reviewed evidence demonstrates measurable deficits in environmental scanning behavior among distracted pedestrians.

title: Pedestrian Behavior Prediction for Safety Systems
text: This section synthesizes findings from 15 papers examining pedestrian behavior prediction models and their application in automated safety systems, including collision avoidance and adaptive warning mechanisms. The comparative evidence matrix structures claims, metrics, and limitations to support structured analysis of prediction accuracy and system performance. A key observation is that prediction models demonstrate reasonable short-term accuracy but exhibit degraded performance under novel or edge-case scenarios. The literature identifies trade-offs between model complexity, computational requirements, and generalization capability as persistent challenges for deployment in real-time safety systems.

title: Research Gaps and Future Directions
text: The comparative analysis of 15 papers reveals persistent gaps that current approaches have not adequately resolved. These gaps are documented through verified evidence from the review matrix and are categorized by severity below. Gap [G1] (medium severity): Insufficient validation of crossing behavior models across diverse pedestrian populations and environmental contexts. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. Gap [G2] (medium severity): Lack of standardized metric reporting conventions that impede cross-study comparison. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. Gap [G3] (medium severity): Limited integration of psychological determinants within quantitative behavioral models. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. Gap [G4] (medium severity): Inadequate representation of emerging technologies, including autonomous vehicles, in existing crossing behavior frameworks. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. Gap [G5] (medium severity): Insufficient longitudinal data on crossing behavior adaptation and long-term safety outcomes. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. These gaps collectively indicate that the field lacks the empirical foundation needed to support confident generalization of crossing behavior models across contexts, populations, and technologies. Prioritizing the most severe gaps in future empirical work will be essential for advancing both scientific understanding and the practical applicability of crossing behavior models.
